tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst. Zdecyduj, ktore zdania (1.1-1.8) sa zgodne z trescia tekstu (T – True), ktore sa niezgodne (F – False), a ktore zawieraja informacje niepodane w tekscie (NI – No Information).
tasks.text:
VOLCANOES AND EARTHQUAKES

The surface of the Earth may seem solid and still, but beneath our feet, the planet is constantly moving. The Earth's outer layer, called the crust, is broken into large pieces known as tectonic plates. These plates float on a layer of hot, semi-liquid rock called the mantle. The plates move very slowly — usually only a few centimetres per year — but over millions of years, their movement has shaped the continents and oceans we see today.

Volcanoes and earthquakes are closely connected to the movement of tectonic plates. Most volcanoes are found along the edges of plates, especially around the Pacific Ocean in an area called the Ring of Fire. The Ring of Fire stretches for about 40,000 kilometres and contains approximately 75 percent of the world's active volcanoes. When two plates move apart or push against each other, hot melted rock called magma can rise to the surface. Once magma reaches the surface, it is called lava.

There are different types of volcanoes. Some erupt violently, throwing ash and rock high into the air, while others produce slow-moving rivers of lava. Shield volcanoes, like those in Hawaii, have gentle slopes and produce flowing lava. Stratovolcanoes, such as Mount Fuji in Japan, are tall and steep with explosive eruptions. Some volcanoes are active, meaning they could erupt at any time. Others are dormant, meaning they have not erupted for a long time but could become active again.

Earthquakes happen when tectonic plates suddenly slip past each other, releasing energy in the form of seismic waves. The strength of an earthquake is measured using the Richter scale. Earthquakes that measure below 3.0 on the Richter scale are usually too weak to be felt by people. However, earthquakes above 7.0 can cause terrible destruction, including collapsed buildings, landslides, and tsunamis — giant ocean waves triggered by underwater earthquakes.

Scientists called seismologists study earthquakes and volcanoes using special instruments. While they cannot predict exactly when an earthquake will happen, they can identify areas that are most at risk and help communities prepare for these powerful natural events.

items.statement: Tectonic plates move several metres each year.
The Ring of Fire is located around the Pacific Ocean.
About 75 percent of all active volcanoes are found in the Ring of Fire.
The deadliest volcanic eruption in history occurred in Indonesia.
Shield volcanoes typically have steep slopes and violent eruptions.
Mount Fuji is an example of a stratovolcano.
Japan experiences more earthquakes per year than any other country in the world.
Seismologists can now predict the exact date of an earthquake several days in advance.
